problem_summary: 2023 ICM Problem F (filed here under MCM Problem D / operations_research): build a network model of the relationships among the UN's 17 Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), then use it to (1) describe inter-goal relationships, (2) evaluate which single SDG to prioritize and which goals are achievable in 10 years, (3) study how completing one SDG reshapes priorities and propose new goals, (4) model how international events/crises perturb the network and priorities, and (5) generalize the method to other organizations. This paper's answer is a data-driven multilayer-network engine: it builds 17 Goal-Target-Indicator-Data (G-T-I-D) networks from real World Bank time series, stitches them together via Pearson correlations into a single 17x17 inter-SDG adjacency matrix G=A_gg, and then runs matrix-iteration scoring on G to drive every downstream decision.
models_used: Multilayer Complex Network (MCN) of SDGs: four node layers Ng(17 goals)/Nt(169 targets)/Ni(241 indicators)/Nd(500 World Bank data series), with the official UN G-T-I hierarchy giving A_gt[17x169], A_ti[169x241], A_id[241x500]
Pearson correlation coefficient matrix on the 500 data series to build A_dd (edges kept only when r>0.9 at p<0.1 significance), the only empirical/data-driven layer

Chained matrix-product collapse to derive the inter-goal adjacency matrix: A_ii=A_id·A_dd·A_di, A_tt=A_ti·A_id·A_dd·A_di·A_it, A_gg=A_gt·A_ti·A_id·A_dd·A_di·A_it·A_tg — projecting 500-D data correlations up to a 17x17 signed/weighted SDG network G

System-dynamics / linear iteration priority-scoring model: x_n = A^n·x_0 (x = 17-D implementation vector in [0,1]); score y = ||A_gg^n||_1 (L1 norm of the n-step propagated influence) used to rank which SDG to prioritize; n=10 used as a '10-year' horizon for achievability

Directed-graph modification (Sub-model III): once an SDG is 'achieved', its bidirectional edges in A_gg become unidirectional (completed goal still influences others, others no longer influence it), producing A_gg' and re-scored priorities

Perturbation/linear-algebra crisis model (Sub-model IV): G'=G+ΔG with ΔG=c·Δx, derived from x1=x0·G, x2=x0·G', x2=x1+Δx ⇒ G'=G+x0^{-1}·Δx, where Δx is a 1x17 event-impact (perturbation) vector and c is an anti-interference/resistance coefficient tied to current state x0
Hand-built 1x17 perturbation matrices Δx for 6 event types (technological advances, global epidemics, climate change, regional wars, refugee movement, natural disasters) as crisis scenario inputs
key_techniques: Reusing the UN's own Goal-Target-Indicator taxonomy as the fixed scaffold (A_gt, A_ti) and only learning the bottom data layer from correlations — anchors the model in authoritative structure while staying data-driven
Matrix-multiplication 'projection' to collapse a huge sparse 500-node data graph down to an interpretable 17x17 goal graph (rather than hand-assigning inter-goal weights from expert opinion)
Encoding sign as meaning: positive vs negative A_gg entries = mutually supportive vs antagonistic SDG pairs, color-coded (orange-red = support, light-blue = conflict) with brightness = magnitude
Single reusable scoring primitive (powers of A_gg + L1 norm) answering four different questions: best priority, 10-year achievability, post-completion re-prioritization, and crisis re-prioritization
Regional generalization as built-in validation: rebuilding G separately for 10 World Bank regions (WLD, OED, ARB, CEB, EAS, ECS, EUU, LCN, MEA, NAC) and checking results match real-world intuition
Closed-form crisis update G'=G+x0^{-1}·Δx that keeps the same scoring machinery but injects an exogenous shock vector
Treating goal completion as a graph-topology edit (bidirectional→unidirectional) rather than just a value change
why_it_won: "It is a genuinely data-driven, end-to-end coherent system rather than a pile of disconnected sub-answers. One artifact — the 17x17 inter-SDG matrix G derived from real World Bank time series via correlation + matrix projection — feeds every one of the five questions through a single, repeatedly-reused scoring operator (powers of G plus an L1 norm). Judges reward exactly this kind of unifying thread. The model also stays anchored to authoritative structure (the UN's own G-T-I hierarchy) while improving on prior expert-only network studies it explicitly cites (Sebestyén et al.), giving it both novelty and credibility. It validates by rebuilding the network for 10 distinct regions and showing the priorities track real-world differences (e.g. NAC needs climate goals, MEA needs poverty/hunger goals), which doubles as a robustness story. Each sub-model is a clean, named incremental edit to G (directed-edge conversion for completion; additive perturbation G'=G+c·Δx for crises), so the modeling reads as one escalating narrative. Finally it is honest: an explicit Pros/Cons section names the linearity assumption and the r>0.9 data-pruning as limitations, which signals mature self-critique to judges."
section_highlights: "Structure is the strongest writing asset: a one-page 'Our Work' flowchart (Figure 1) maps the whole pipeline up front, and the body is organized as Sub-model I→IV each mapped to one problem part, so a judge can navigate by problem. The Summary states concrete, quantified findings per problem (e.g. 'SDG8 positively affects 14 SDGs, SDG17 negatively affects 16'; 'SDG4 recommended in 5 regions'; 'SDG1 achievable in 9 regions within 10 years'; 'under war the optimal shifts from SDG1 to SDG17') rather than vague claims. Heavy, varied, decision-oriented visualization: signed/colored network graphs of the SDG relationships, per-region effectiveness bar charts (Figure 9: SDG4 scores 10/10 in WLD), a cross-region line chart (Figure 11), a 10-region × goal achievability heatmap (Figure 12), before/after priority comparison bars (Figure 14), and a radar + stacked bar for crisis impact. Notation table and explicit Assumptions-with-justification section are present. The progressive robustness section tests each sub-model on its own axis (region, SDG order, time length, perturbation). The Problem-5 generalization is made concrete with a worked company example (market/innovation/profit/social goals decomposed into the same G-T-I-D layering)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Correlation treated as causal influence: A_dd is pure Pearson correlation, yet the paper interprets signed G entries as one SDG 'causing' positive/negative effects on another and even as feedback — no causal identification, no lag structure despite using 1967–2018 time series

Hard r>0.9 (p<0.1) threshold discards most data and any non-linear coupling; the paper itself flags this in Cons, but it likely makes G sparse and threshold-sensitive (no sensitivity sweep on the 0.9 cutoff is shown)

Linearity assumed throughout (x_n=A^n x_0); for a contraction-like A this iteration can collapse toward 0 or blow up, and the ad hoc clamping ('if coefficient>1 treat as 1, if <0 treat as 0') is mathematically unjustified and breaks the linear algebra

The crisis derivation G'=G+x0^{-1}·Δx requires x0 (a 1x17 vector) to be 'invertible', which is not well-defined for a row vector; the algebra around x0^{-1} and the coefficient c is hand-wavy and the meaning of c is asserted, not derived or fitted

Several reproduced figures are internally inconsistent / mislabeled (e.g. two different figures both captioned 'using WLD as an example'; radar legend says 'Region Wars' while text says natural disasters; placeholder SDG18–SDG50 nodes in a mermaid chart), suggesting rushed figure production and weak proofreading
Perturbation vectors Δx for the 6 events are assigned 'based on common sense' with no data backing — a subjective input driving the headline crisis conclusions
n=10 powers of A is equated with '10 years' with no calibration that one matrix iteration equals one year; the time semantics are asserted not validated
Assumption that the relationship network is static over time directly contradicts using 50 years of time-series data and the crisis sub-model
reusable_patterns: Layered-taxonomy-to-adjacency projection: when an authoritative hierarchy exists (goals→targets→indicators→data, or any concept→metric→raw-data tree), reuse it as fixed structural matrices and only learn the bottom layer from data, then collapse upward with chained matrix products A_top = A_top,...·A_data,data·...·A_top to get an interpretable small adjacency matrix

One scoring operator, many questions: define a single propagation score (here y=||A^n x||_1) and reuse it verbatim for ranking, achievability (large n), and post-change re-ranking — strong unifying device judges reward
Encode relationship sign + magnitude as color hue + brightness in network plots so a reader instantly reads 'who helps/hurts whom and how much'

Model an intervention as an explicit edit to the network object, not just a number: completion ⇒ make edges directed; external shock ⇒ additive perturbation G'=G+c·Δx — gives each problem-part a clean, nameable mechanism
Use a one-page 'Our Work' pipeline flowchart as the first figure to give judges a navigable map of the whole solution

Validate-by-generalization: rebuild the same model across many sub-populations (here 10 regions) and show the differences are real-world plausible — doubles as both an answer and a robustness argument
Make the Problem-5 'generalize your method' part concrete with a fully worked alternate domain (company strategic goals decomposed through the identical layering), not just a paragraph of hand-waving
Always include an explicit Pros/Cons (self-critique) section naming the modeling assumptions you knowingly made — signals maturity even when the assumptions are strong
